title: Styphelia coelophylla
taxonomy: Styphelia coelophylla is classified within the family Ericaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the heath family. This family includes many genera and species, such as Rhododendron and Calluna. Styphelia coelophylla is native to Australia and is part of the Styphelia genus, which contains about 40 species of small shrubs and trees. The Ericaceae family is further divided into several subfamilies and tribes, with Styphelia coelophylla being part of the Epacridoideae subfamily. Styphelia coelophylla's classification helps to understand its evolutionary relationships and ecological characteristics.

Styphelia coelophylla has been found to have potential therapeutic properties, including antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. intro: Styphelia coelophylla, also known as Tasmanian tea tree, is a small shrub native to Australia, belonging to the family Ericaceae. The leaves of this plant have been traditionally used in herbal medicine, offering various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in soothing skin irritations, wounds, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Styphelia coelophylla include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Styphelia coelophylla is often made into a tea, tincture, or infusion, which is used to treat a range of health conditions, including colds, fever, and digestive issues.
